title: SAP Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture.
plain_summary: The SAP Act of 2025 makes changes to a government program that helps maple syrup producers with grants for research and education. The biggest change requires the Department of Agriculture to ask maple industry people what kind of research and education they need most before deciding who gets grants. The bill also extends this maple syrup support program until 2030. This program gives money to states, tribal governments, and research groups to help grow the domestic maple syrup industry and keep it sustainable.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Supporting All Producers Act of twenty twenty five or the SAP Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Acer Access and Development Program
sections.text: This section makes changes to the Acer access and development program that was created in the Agricultural Act of twenty fourteen. The Acer program helps maple syrup producers with research and education. The changes work like this. First, the law moves some existing parts of the program to new locations in the text. Second, it adds a new requirement called consultations. Under this new rule, the Secretary of Agriculture must ask maple industry stakeholders what their research and education priorities are. Stakeholders means people who work in the maple syrup business like farmers and producers. The Secretary must ask for this input at least six months before asking for grant applications. This consultation requirement starts with the first grant application process that happens at least one year after this SAP Act becomes law. When the Secretary decides which grants to give out, they must think about the information they got from the maple industry people. Third, the law extends the program until twenty thirty instead of twenty twenty three.
